original_abstract: Although the oxidative addition of Ni(I) to aryl iodides is a frequently invoked step in catalytic cycles, a comprehensive mechanistic foundation for this fundamental transformation remains elusive. In this work, we present an in-depth mechanistic investigation of this process employing a combination of electroanalytical methods and statistical modeling. Through the use of electrochemistry, we rapidly quantified oxidative addition rates across a broad library of aryl iodide substrates and four structurally distinct, catalytically relevant ligand classes (Ni(MeBPy), Ni(MePhen), Ni(Terpy), and Ni(BPP)). Leveraging a dataset of more than 200 experimental rate measurements, we utilized multivariate linear regression to isolate the key electronic and steric parameters governing reactivity. Our findings reveal a mechanistic bifurcation where the pathway—either a concerted three-center transition state or a halogen-atom abstraction—is dictated primarily by the ligand architecture. Finally, we developed a global heatmap of predicted oxidative addition rates, demonstrating its utility in rationalizing reaction outcomes through a case study of a Ni-catalyzed cross-coupling transformation.
humanized_abstract: The oxidative addition of Ni(I) to aryl iodides is a widely accepted if poorly understood step in many catalytic cross-coupling reactions. Here, we present a detailed understanding of the mechanism of this process, obtained by combining the power of electrochemistry with advanced statistical modeling to understand how different aryl iodides react under identical conditions, and how different ligands on the catalytic Ni(I) center influence the rates of oxidative addition. We determined oxidative addition rates to a panel of aryl iodides for four different ligand classes that are relevant to catalysis: Ni(MeBPy), Ni(MePhen), Ni(Terpy), and Ni(BPP) using electrolysis, and then used a data set of over 200 rates to carry out a multivariate linear regression that isolated the electronic and steric effects that determine the rates of oxidative addition and mapped out a global heatmap of rates that can be used to understand reactivity in Ni cross-coupling reactions exemplified with a Ni-catalyzed cross-coupling reaction.
